content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing a game on a sequence of NNN positive integers, say A1,A2,...ANA_1, A_2, ... A_NA1​,A2​,...AN​ The game is played as follows.


If all the numbers are equal, the game ends.
Otherwise

Select two numbers which are unequal
Subtract the smaller number from the larger number
Replace the larger number with the result from above


Chef has already figured out that the game **always terminates**. He also knows, for a given sequence of integers, the game will always terminate on the **same value**, no matter how the game is played. Chef wants you to simulate the game for him and tell him if the game terminates on 111.

In fact, there may be many such games. Given a sequence of integers Chef wants to know the number of **sub-sequences** of the given sequence, for which, playing the above game on the subsuquence will terminate on 111. A **sub-sequence** can be obtained from the original sequence by **deleting 000 or more** integers from the original sequence. See the explanation section for clarity.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines. The first line contains a single integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N positive integers, each separated by a single space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of sub-sequences of the original sequence, for which, playing the game on the sub-sequence results in ending the game with all the values equal to 1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
There are 16 sub-sequences that have a GCD of 1.

Explanation:
There is only 1 sub-sequence, which is {1} and it has a GCD of 1.

solution.approach: The key insight is to understand that the game will always terminate on the same value, which is the GCD of all the numbers in the sequence. We can use this property to count the number of sub-sequences that will terminate on 1. We generate all possible sub-sequences and calculate their GCD. If the GCD is 1, we increment the count.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 2^N * N) per test case, O(T * 2^N * N) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the array to store the sub-sequence.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
# Uses input() or sys.stdin
# Handles all T test cases
import sys
import math

def gcd(a, b):
    if b == 0: return a
    return gcd(b, a % b)

def playGame(arr):
    count = 0
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(1 << n):
        subSeq = [arr[j] for j in range(n) if (i & (1 << j)) != 0]
        if subSeq:
            g = subSeq[0]
            for j in range(1, len(subSeq)):
                g = gcd(g, subSeq[j])
            if g == 1: count += 1
    return count

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
    print(playGame(arr))
